title: Using smart plugs to map household energy draws
subtitle: A systematic approach to measuring which appliances use the most electricity
excerpt: A whole-house energy monitor tells you how much electricity the house is using. A smart plug tells you which appliance is using it. For a typical UK household with a dozen plug-in appliances, a week with two or three smart plugs identifies the largest consumers and quantifies the savings from replacing or adjusting them.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust UK household appliance energy use guidance. DECC (now BEIS) Household Energy Use in England study. Which? appliance energy testing data. Smart Energy GB guidance on monitoring consumption.

projectSchedule.title: Review the first week of readings
projectSchedule.body: After the first week of monitoring, review the kWh figures from each plug. Sort by total energy consumed. The highest-consuming appliances are the ones where change will have the most impact.

content.text: A smart plug with energy monitoring costs 8-15 pounds and shows real-time wattage and cumulative kWh in a phone app. Two or three used in rotation over a month will tell you more about where your electricity goes than a year of smart meter bills. The goal is not to monitor everything forever. It is to find the three to five highest-consuming items, make changes, and check the saving.

Start with items that are on for long periods rather than items with high peak wattage. A 2000 W kettle that runs for 3 minutes uses 0.1 kWh. A 60 W fish tank pump running for 8 hours uses 0.48 kWh. Duration, not peak wattage, determines total energy cost.

Monitor the fridge and fridge-freezer for 48 hours. These cycle on and off; the smart plug shows average consumption including compressor-off time. A well-maintained 10-year-old fridge-freezer typically uses 350-600 kWh per year; a modern A-rated unit uses 100-200 kWh per year. The potential saving from replacement is 150-400 kWh annually.

Monitor the chest freezer if you have one. A chest freezer in an unheated garage uses 200-500 kWh per year depending on its age and condition. If it holds mostly air for much of the year, the question is whether its contents justify the running cost.

Monitor the television setup (TV plus box plus soundbar) during an evening. Modern LED TVs draw 50-120 W while streaming; their set-top boxes often draw 15-25 W continuously, even in standby. At 27p/kWh, a box drawing 20 W for 20 hours per day costs 53 pounds per year.

Monitor electric heaters in rooms that are heated independently of the central heating. A 2 kW electric convector running for 4 hours costs 2.16 pounds per day at 27p/kWh, or 540 pounds over a six-month heating season.

Monitor the home office setup over a working day. A desktop PC, monitor, and printer in active use draw 100-400 W. On standby all night, they may draw 10-40 W. A managed power strip that cuts standby power when the PC goes to sleep can save 80-200 kWh per year.
Plug the smart plug into the first appliance socket and reset its kWh counter to zero. Run for exactly 7 days. Note the kWh reading and the appliance.
Move to the next appliance. Continue down the priority list above, spending a full week on each main consumer.
After three to four weeks of monitoring, rank the appliances by annual kWh (weekly reading multiplied by 52). Focus your attention on the top three.

Annual kWh x 0.27 = annual cost in pounds at 2025/26 rates. If an ageing fridge-freezer uses 480 kWh per year and a replacement uses 150 kWh per year, the saving is 330 kWh, roughly 89 pounds per year. A replacement might cost 400-600 pounds. Payback: 4-7 years, within the expected life of the new appliance.

Do not replace appliances that are still working. Manufacturing a new appliance has a real carbon cost: a fridge-freezer carries roughly 300-500 kg CO2e to produce. If the running-cost saving justifies replacement on financial grounds, the carbon case usually follows within a few years at UK grid intensity.

attrs.symptom: Smart plug shows inconsistent readings for the same appliance on different days
attrs.cause: The appliance load varies with use pattern: a washing machine draws very differently on a boil wash vs a cold cycle
attrs.fix: Log the specific programme and load used each time. Compare like-for-like runs rather than averaging across all cycles.

attrs.symptom: Smart plug total across all monitored appliances is less than the smart meter whole-house total
attrs.cause: Large fixed loads (oven, shower, immersion heater, EV charger) are not captured by smart plugs
attrs.fix: Use the smart meter data to estimate the fixed loads. Subtract all smart-plug totals from the meter total for the same period. The remainder is your unmonitored hardwired loads.
